title: Why This Blog Doesn't Have Comments
summary: I don't actually get asked this at all, but people ask me a lot, "why doesn't your blog have comments?" It's simple. Comments foster hostility, often provide no contributions to the article, and social media works fine for comments. Hostility Anyone who actually reads the comments section of a news article, I feel sorry for you. It often tends to be a cessp…
content:
I don't actually get asked this at all, but people ask me a lot, "why doesn't your blog have comments?" It's simple. Comments foster hostility, often provide no contributions to the article, and social media works fine for comments.

Hostility

Anyone who actually reads the comments section of a news article, I feel sorry for you. It often tends to be a cesspool of hate. To quote Anaal Nathrakh's album liner for Endarkenment:

When we heard about this and looked it up, the first information we found was on the website of a reactionary newspaper, well-known in the UK. Contrasting with Barney’s humanistic appeal, we scrolled down and read some of the user comments. Many people say ‘Never read the comments!’, but that would miss out on so much that is quintessentially human. People can be so endearing. Sometimes you just want to vomit blood on them.

The comments made were every bit as compassionate as you might expect. So we made an audio collage of readings of them and put it in the background of the song – it’s layered up to the point of being incomprehensible, but those of you who read this will know what you’re hearing. Essentially, it’s a condensed, pathetic form of the two minutes of hate.

And it is. If you don't believe me, go to any mildly controversial news article / blog post / social media post and read a few of the comments. I'm not going to link examples here because I appreciate this website and don't want to smear that filth here.

Useless

Comments are useless. They offer no contribution to the article itself. It is just a place for people of offer their opinions. Rarely are they used to correct something in the article. If you need to correct something, feel free to contact me. That is how it ought to be. Comments are a terrible medium to foster discussion and often get lost in the pond of other useless information.

Then, there is spam.

As literally nobody reads this blog, the only comments I would get are along the lines of "CLICK HERE TO GET FREE MONEY 100% GARANTEED NO SCAM NO HAC". And I have. On some of my other websites where I didn't have the foresight to disable comments (WordPress had them by default, what was I supposed to do?), I got nothing but spam and SEO marketing. Useless.

Next, comments often devolve into bikeshedding arguments and don't actually do anything except for perhaps starting a flame war. My website isn't social media. Take your Vim vs Emacs "discussion" elsewhere.

Social Media

If you really want to discuss my article with strangers, put it on Hacker News, Twitter, or whatever else you want. That's fine. They have better comment policies and more moderators than I do anyway. Offering that little extra bit of difficulty in voicing your opinion is a great way to filter out people who want to do nothing more than cause issues.

I appreciate HN because the comments section doesn't often devolve into a cesspool of hate. Civilized discussion is fine. Just keep it off of my site. It causes a lot more issues than it solves.

Conclusion

Don't put comments on your website. It's a waste of your time. A few news sites are getting rid of them (good riddance) and Grav is wonderful because it doesn't enable comments by default.

As always, if you find something incorrect with this article, feel free to contact me.
